evals.prompt: I want to build a Claude-powered code review bot that my CI/CD pipeline can call via the API. It should read pull request diffs, check for security vulnerabilities, and return a structured JSON report. Walk me through how to build this.
evals.expected_output: Identifies Managed Agent as the right primitive, directs to grounding-managed-agents.md, references the managed-agent.md template, covers validation gates (validate.sh, audit.py, compile.py), warns against always_allow, and mentions pinning skill versions.
assertions: Identifies Managed Agent as the correct primitive (API-callable from external code)
Directs to read references/grounding-managed-agents.md
References the managed-agent.md template
Mentions the validation gate sequence: validate.sh, audit.py, compile.py
Warns against using always_allow broadly or for agent_toolset
Mentions pinning skill versions rather than using 'latest'

evals.prompt: I'm building a skill for code analysis. During long sessions I want to spawn a parallel background worker that searches across a large codebase for dependency issues while I keep working. How do I set up this kind of agent in Claude Code?
evals.expected_output: Identifies Claude Code subagent (or agent team with background flag) as the right primitive, references grounding-claude-code.md, recommends typed subagent over general-purpose, mentions validation with audit.py or validate.sh.
assertions: Identifies Claude Code subagent or agent team as the correct primitive (spawned during a session / parallel workers)
References grounding-claude-code.md
Recommends using a typed subagent or agent team rather than general-purpose
Mentions validation using audit.py or validate.sh

evals.prompt: I want Claude to automatically check git diff output after every bash command and warn me if any files with 'credentials' or 'secrets' in their name were modified. Should I create an agent for this?
evals.expected_output: Correctly identifies this as a hook use case (behavior shaping only, no new context needed), recommends Skill+hooks primitive NOT a full agent, suggests a PostToolUse hook, does NOT direct to managed-agent or subagent templates.
assertions: Correctly identifies this as a Skill+hooks use case (behavior shaping only) rather than an agent
Recommends a hook (PostToolUse or similar) rather than building a full agent
Does NOT recommend building a Managed Agent or Claude Code subagent for this use case
Mentions the hook-development skill for implementation details

evals.prompt: I need to add a web search tool to my existing Managed Agent. Here's my update call: `agents.update(agent_id='code-review-agent', tools=[{'type': 'web_search'}])`. This looks right to me—what do you think?
evals.expected_output: Warns about the wholesale replacement anti-pattern, explains that omitting existing tools from the array silently deletes them, advises to include complete existing tools array, suggests running diff.py before deploying.
assertions: Warns that agents.update replaces the tools array wholesale (not merging)
Explains that omitting existing tools from the update call silently deletes them
Advises including all existing tools in the complete array alongside the new tool
Mentions running diff.py before deploying to catch silent deletions

evals.prompt: I've finished updating my Managed Agent's system prompt and adding a new GitHub MCP server to it. I'm ready to push to production. What steps should I take to make sure I don't accidentally break anything?
evals.expected_output: Mentions running diff.py before deploy, explains exit code 2 means silent deletion, requires explicit acknowledgment of deletions, mentions audit.py for permission validation.
assertions: Mentions running diff.py before the update as mandatory
Explains that exit code 2 from diff.py means a silent deletion will occur
Requires explicit acknowledgment of each deletion before proceeding
Mentions audit.py for permission/safety validation
Does NOT suggest skipping the diff.py check

evals.prompt: I've built my first Claude Code subagent for handling database schema migrations. How should I test it properly to make sure it behaves correctly and won't surprise me in production?
evals.expected_output: References testing-patterns.md, mentions Layer A (hook-based assertions/deterministic) and Layer B (LLM-as-judge for quality), mentions simulate.py with --sandbox flag, covers flakiness and latency/cost reporting.
assertions: References or instructs to read references/testing-patterns.md
Mentions Layer A testing: hook-based assertions (deterministic)
Mentions Layer B testing: LLM-as-judge for qualitative checks
Mentions simulate.py as the testing command
Mentions running with --sandbox flag for safety
